title: Intro to trees in R
text:
This page is basically following the vignette Ancestral.html for the phangorn package in R.
The original vignette is written by Klaus Schliep (klaus.schliep@gmail.com).
I’m annotating it a bit more for our purposes.
First, let’s install some of the packages we will need:

library(phangorn)
library(phytools)
library(ape)

Next, let’s load in some data. We will read in an example dataset from the phangorn package in R, which contains genetic sequence information about a number of different species.

#Let's load in some primate data:
fdir <- system.file("extdata/trees", package = "phangorn")
primates <- read.phyDat(file.path(fdir, "primates.dna"),
                        format = "interleaved")

Let’s examine this data file - what does it contain?

names(primates)

 [1] "Mouse"      "Bovine"     "Lemur"      "Tarsier"    "Squir Monk"
 [6] "Jpn Macaq"  "Rhesus Mac" "Crab-E.Mac" "BarbMacaq"  "Gibbon"    
[11] "Orang"      "Gorilla"    "Chimp"      "Human"     


This shows us there are 14 sequences corresponding to 14 different species. Let’s look at Mouse:

primates$Mouse

  [1] 1 2 2 1 1 1 1 1 1 1 2 1 4 2 2 1 1 1 2 1 2 1 1 2 2 2 2 1 3 2 2 2 4 4 2 3 2
 [38] 1 1 4 3 2 2 1 4 2 1 3 1 1 4 1 4 4 1 4 1 2 4 1 2 4 1 1 1 1 1 2 4 2 1 1 1 4
 [75] 4 1 1 2 4 2 4 4 4 1 1 4 2 4 4 4 1 4 1 2 1 1 2 1 4 4 2 2 1 2 2 1 1 2 2 4 1
[112] 4 2 2 1 2 1 2 1 1 1 1 1 2 4 2 1 4 1 4 4 4 1 4 2 4 1 1 1 4 1 2 3 1 1 2 4 4
[149] 2 1 2 1 2 1 1 2 2 4 4 1 2 1 2 1 4 1 1 1 2 1 4 2 2 2 2 1 3 2 2 1 1 2 1 2 2
[186] 2 4 4 2 2 1 2 1 1 1 4 2 4 4 4 1 4 1 2 3 2 2 2 1 4 1 1 1 4 1 1 2


It’s a sequence of the numbers 1–4. Typing primates into the console indicates that the states are a,c,g,t:

primates

14 sequences with 232 character and 217 different site patterns.
The states are a c g t 


So the Mouse sequence is really

c('a','c','t','g')[primates$Mouse]

  [1] "a" "c" "c" "a" "a" "a" "a" "a" "a" "a" "c" "a" "g" "c" "c" "a" "a" "a"
 [19] "c" "a" "c" "a" "a" "c" "c" "c" "c" "a" "t" "c" "c" "c" "g" "g" "c" "t"
 [37] "c" "a" "a" "g" "t" "c" "c" "a" "g" "c" "a" "t" "a" "a" "g" "a" "g" "g"
 [55] "a" "g" "a" "c" "g" "a" "c" "g" "a" "a" "a" "a" "a" "c" "g" "c" "a" "a"
 [73] "a" "g" "g" "a" "a" "c" "g" "c" "g" "g" "g" "a" "a" "g" "c" "g" "g" "g"
 [91] "a" "g" "a" "c" "a" "a" "c" "a" "g" "g" "c" "c" "a" "c" "c" "a" "a" "c"
[109] "c" "g" "a" "g" "c" "c" "a" "c" "a" "c" "a" "a" "a" "a" "a" "c" "g" "c"
[127] "a" "g" "a" "g" "g" "g" "a" "g" "c" "g" "a" "a" "a" "g" "a" "c" "t" "a"
[145] "a" "c" "g" "g" "c" "a" "c" "a" "c" "a" "a" "c" "c" "g" "g" "a" "c" "a"
[163] "c" "a" "g" "a" "a" "a" "c" "a" "g" "c" "c" "c" "c" "a" "t" "c" "c" "a"
[181] "a" "c" "a" "c" "c" "c" "g" "g" "c" "c" "a" "c" "a" "a" "a" "g" "c" "g"
[199] "g" "g" "a" "g" "a" "c" "t" "c" "c" "c" "a" "g" "a" "a" "a" "g" "a" "a"
[217] "c"


The letters “a”, “c”, “t”, and “g” correspond to the different nucleotides in deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA). If you’ve never heard of DNA, say hello to the bugs (the ones living there under the rock with you).
Let’s turn this species data into an R data frame to visualize the sequence alignment. Columns correspond to species, and rows correspond to a position in the DNA sequence:

primdat <- as.data.frame(primates)

Let’s look at the the beginning of the multiple sequence alignment:

head(primdat)

  Mouse Bovine Lemur Tarsier Squir Monk Jpn Macaq Rhesus Mac Crab-E.Mac
1     a      a     a       a          a         a          a          a
2     c      c     c       t          c         c          c          c
3     c      c     c       c          c         t          t          c
4     a      a     a       t          c         c          t          c
5     a      a     a       a          c         c          c          c
6     a      a     a       c          a         a          a          a
  BarbMacaq Gibbon Orang Gorilla Chimp Human
1         a      a     a       a     a     a
2         c      c     c       c     c     c
3         c      t     c       c     c     c
4         c      a     c       c     c     c
5         t      t     c       c     c     c
6         a      a     a       a     a     a


The sequences appear to be mostly similar. If we had included bugs or plants in the data, there might be more obvious differences. The fact that the sequences are rather similar among these species indicates that they share ancestry somewhat recently (at least in the grand scheme of life on Earth as we know it).
Let’s look at each pair of species in the data and find the number of differences in their sequences.
For example, if we wanted to see how many sites differ between Mouse and Human, we could do this:

sum(primdat$Mouse != primdat$Human)

[1] 128


128 sites differ between these species; there are

dim(primdat)[1]

[1] 232


total sites in each sequence.
We will load these into a matrix, where the i,j entry tells us how many sites differ between species i and species j.
This matrix will obviously be symmetric, and the diagonal will be zero. S So, we can can just fill in the upper diagonal of the matrix to get the information we need, and then use symmetry to fill in the lower half:

seq_diff_matrix <- matrix(nrow=14,ncol=14)
colnames(seq_diff_matrix) <- names(primates)
rownames(seq_diff_matrix) <- names(primates)

diag(seq_diff_matrix) <- 0

#I will swallow my pride and write a nested for loop (real R pros use the apply family of functions):
for(i in 1:14){
    for(j in i:14){
        seq_diff_matrix[i,j] <- sum( primdat[,i] != primdat[,j] )
    }
}

seq_diff_matrix[lower.tri(seq_diff_matrix)] <- t(seq_diff_matrix)[lower.tri(t(seq_diff_matrix))]

#check to make sure I did this right -- is the matrix equal to its transpose? (i.e. is it symmetric?)
seq_diff_matrix == t(seq_diff_matrix)

           Mouse Bovine Lemur Tarsier Squir Monk Jpn Macaq Rhesus Mac
Mouse       TRUE   TRUE  TRUE    TRUE       TRUE      TRUE       TRUE
Bovine      TRUE   TRUE  TRUE    TRUE       TRUE      TRUE       TRUE
Lemur       TRUE   TRUE  TRUE    TRUE       TRUE      TRUE       TRUE
Tarsier     TRUE   TRUE  TRUE    TRUE       TRUE      TRUE       TRUE
Squir Monk  TRUE   TRUE  TRUE    TRUE       TRUE      TRUE       TRUE
Jpn Macaq   TRUE   TRUE  TRUE    TRUE       TRUE      TRUE       TRUE
Rhesus Mac  TRUE   TRUE  TRUE    TRUE       TRUE      TRUE       TRUE
Crab-E.Mac  TRUE   TRUE  TRUE    TRUE       TRUE      TRUE       TRUE
BarbMacaq   TRUE   TRUE  TRUE    TRUE       TRUE      TRUE       TRUE
Gibbon      TRUE   TRUE  TRUE    TRUE       TRUE      TRUE       TRUE
Orang       TRUE   TRUE  TRUE    TRUE       TRUE      TRUE       TRUE
Gorilla     TRUE   TRUE  TRUE    TRUE       TRUE      TRUE       TRUE
Chimp       TRUE   TRUE  TRUE    TRUE       TRUE      TRUE       TRUE
Human       TRUE   TRUE  TRUE    TRUE       TRUE      TRUE       TRUE
           Crab-E.Mac BarbMacaq Gibbon Orang Gorilla Chimp Human
Mouse            TRUE      TRUE   TRUE  TRUE    TRUE  TRUE  TRUE
Bovine           TRUE      TRUE   TRUE  TRUE    TRUE  TRUE  TRUE
Lemur            TRUE      TRUE   TRUE  TRUE    TRUE  TRUE  TRUE
Tarsier          TRUE      TRUE   TRUE  TRUE    TRUE  TRUE  TRUE
Squir Monk       TRUE      TRUE   TRUE  TRUE    TRUE  TRUE  TRUE
Jpn Macaq        TRUE      TRUE   TRUE  TRUE    TRUE  TRUE  TRUE
Rhesus Mac       TRUE      TRUE   TRUE  TRUE    TRUE  TRUE  TRUE
Crab-E.Mac       TRUE      TRUE   TRUE  TRUE    TRUE  TRUE  TRUE
BarbMacaq        TRUE      TRUE   TRUE  TRUE    TRUE  TRUE  TRUE
Gibbon           TRUE      TRUE   TRUE  TRUE    TRUE  TRUE  TRUE
Orang            TRUE      TRUE   TRUE  TRUE    TRUE  TRUE  TRUE
Gorilla          TRUE      TRUE   TRUE  TRUE    TRUE  TRUE  TRUE
Chimp            TRUE      TRUE   TRUE  TRUE    TRUE  TRUE  TRUE
Human            TRUE      TRUE   TRUE  TRUE    TRUE  TRUE  TRUE

#Yes it is -- now plot!
heatmap(seq_diff_matrix, symm=T)








So already, it is abundantly clear that Humans, Chimps, and Gorillas are are pretty similar in terms of their DNA sequences. There is also a group in the middle (BarbMacq, Crab-E.Mac, Jpn Macaq, and Rhesus Mac) that look pretty similar to each other.
The heatmap function even makes a nice little dendogram tree on the margins!
We have made a tree, but there are some potential drawbacks with this. The tree should reflect the evolutionary history of these organisms.
That means the internal nodes of the tree should have their own sequences because they correspond to ancestral organisms with their own DNA sequences. In constructing a phylogenetic tree, we should be taking account of the fact that closely related things have shared ancestry, and the ancestor will look similar to its recent descendants.
In this tree we have produced so far, we have only looked at raw differences between pairs of sequences. We have not explicitly made use of the idea of “common descent” from ancestors. The idea of similarity due to common descent means that the location of nucleotide differences in the sequences will matter. Internal nodes of our phylogenetic tree should correspond to ancestors of present-day organisms. These ancestors will have nucleotide sequences of their own, and these should be similar to the sequences in the descendants. We have not accounted for this.
This is why the method of Parsimony was originally developed. Instead of looking at pairwise differences between sequences, we try to determine what the evolutionary history would have been, under the assumption that evolution is “parsimonious” – the number of nucleotide changes from the root of the tree to the leaves should be minimized.
Let’s calculate a tree using Parsimony:

tree <- pratchet(primates, trace=0) |> acctran(primates) |> makeNodeLabel()
 parsimony(tree, primates)

[1] 746

anc.pars <- anc_pars(tree, primates)

#Let's look at the evolution of site 17 in the sequences:
plotAnc(anc.pars, 17)
title("Maximum Parsimony Reconstruction")








The Parsimony reconstruction method provides us with a way to estimate what ancestral sequences might have looked like. Already, we can see that species that are closely related tend to have the same sequence, and so do their ancestors (at least in the recent past).
We can calculate pairwise distances between the species once again, but this time using the branch lengths in the tree. The distance between two species corresponds to the sum of the branch lengths along the unique path connecting the two species through their most recent common ancestor. Because we are dealing with trees (a special type of graph), there is always a unique path connecting two leaves (so that the notion of “distance” is well defined).

parsmny_dist_mat <- cophenetic(tree)

You can verify that this matrix is symmetric. Let’s visualize this:

par(mfrow=c(1,2))
heatmap(parsmny_dist_mat,symm=T)








This is slightly different from the pairwise sequence differene matrix. Let’s compare the pairwise distances in both:

m1 <- seq_diff_matrix
m2 <- parsmny_dist_mat

m1 <- m1[rownames(m2),colnames(m2)]

m1 <- m1[upper.tri(m1)]
m2 <- m2[upper.tri(m2)]

plot(m1,m2,xlab='Nucleotide differences',ylab='Patristic distances (parsimony)')








Clearly these things are correlated - but they are not identical.
Parsimony reconstructions of trees are more biologically grounded than dendograms build from pairwise differences, but we can do even better.
The criterion to minimize the number of nucleotide changes in parsimony ignores back-substitutions that can undo nucleotide changes. Even though such events may be rare in the grand scheme of things, over long timescales they can occur. Ignoring this can lead to spurious conclusions.
This is the motivation for the final tree construction method we will consider here – the so-called Likelihood methods that explicitly model nucleotide substitution as a stochastic process (specifically, as a continuous-time Markov chain). These models depend on parameters that must be estimated from data. As we will see later, Markov chains that describe changes in nucleotides over time may be adapted to model changes in other traits over time (such as location).
Because our focus for this workshop is discrete phylogeography, we will not consider methods making use of continuous data at the tips of trees. It is possible to formulate diffusion models and stochastic processes such as Brownian Motion or the Ornstein-Uhlenbeck process to model evolution of continuous traits from root to leaves. We will not do so here.
Discrete-state Markov models (as well as the Brownian motion models for trait evolution) fall under the category of Likelihood methods for ancestral character estimation. This is because it is possible to fit these models to data using standard techniques from statistics that employ likelihood functions.
The code snippet below will estimate parameters of a discrete-state Markov chain defined on the state space (A,C,G,T) and changing in continuous time. We will not estimate the tree topology. Estimatng parameters of the Markov chain will help us determine the branch lengths in the tree that are most consistent with the data (i.e. the sequences).

fit <- pml(tree, primates)
fit <- optim.pml(fit, model="F81", control = pml.control(trace=0))


#This will give us also the most likely ancestral states:
anc.ml <- anc_pml(fit)

#Let's look at site 17 in the sequences:
plotAnc(anc.ml, 17)
title("Maximum Likelihood Estimate")








Let’s compare the parsimony and ML trees:

par(mfrow=c(1,2))

plot(tree, main='Parsimony tree')

plot(anc.ml$tree, main='Maximum likelihood tree')








The tree topologies (=genealogical relationship) are the same, but the branch lengths are a bit different. Notice, in particular, that the ancestors in the Mouse-Bovine-Tarsier-Lemur clade of the maximum likelihood phylogeny are concentrated together more closely in time than in the parsimony tree.
Let’s calculate pairwise distances in this tree:

mldists <- cophenetic(anc.ml$tree)

c1 <- mldists[upper.tri(mldists)]
c2 <- parsmny_dist_mat[upper.tri(parsmny_dist_mat)]

plot(c1,c2,xlab='Maximum likelihood distances', ylab='Parsimony tree distances')








So the distances between species in the parsimony and Maximum Likelihood tree using the Markov model for nucleotide changes are pretty tightly correlated, but not identical. Hypothesis: the pairwise differences below the diagonal-ish spread of points in the right-hand side of the plot correspond to locations in sequences that experienced a back-mutation (so they are actually separated from each other by a longer period of time than their sequences would otherwise suggest).

title: Intro to trees in R
text:
1. Statistical phylogenetics, in a nutshell
The code example above treats the tree topology (i.e. the geneaology of the organisms) as fixed, and estimates parameters of the Markov chain (which modifies the branch lengths). In the reconstruction of a Maximum likelihood phylogeny, we would need to evaluate the likelihoods of different tree topologies as well as branch lengths in order to produce an estimate of the phylogenetic tree. Surprisingly, tree topology can be treated like a paramter – all we would need to do is optimize our likelihood function over the space of phylogenetic trees.
Use the nni function on the original parsimony tree to obtain all of the trees that are one nearest-neighbor interchange away from the parsimony tree. Fit Markov models to each tree and calculate the likelihood for each tree under the optimized parameters of the Markov model. Then, compare the likelihoods of the different tree topologies. Based off of this, do you think it might be possible to estimate the most likely tree, given the Markov model structure and the sequence data?

title: Intro to trees in R
text:
2.
Load in a different set of sequences from the workshop and try to construct a parsimony tree. Use a nucleotide substitution model of your choice (HKY, F81, etc.) to fit a Markov model describing sequence evolution on the parsimony tree. How much do branch lengths change after fitting the Markov model?

title: Intro to trees in R
text:
3.
Use the read.tree function to load in a tree that you download from someplace. Try plotting it, and if you can find associated metadata (from Nextstrain, for example) try plotting that on the tree as well. It can be handy to combine the plotTree and tiplabels functions from phytools for this purpose.
It is optional, but the ggtree package in R also comes in handy for plotting trees and you might consider installing it.

title: Fitting BiSSE models:
text:
library(diversitree)
library(mcmcensemble)

Now that we have some familiarity with the behavior of the BISSE model, we will try to use the model for parameter estimation, i.e. inference via model fitting.
Our goals are:

Learn how to specify a BiSSE model log-likelihood
Learn how to fit a BiSSE model
Learn how to constrain models to simplify models/estimate fewer parameters
Develop an intuition for how well we can estimate parameters in practice



Simulate a BiSSE tree:

pars <- c(
        lambda0=1,
        lambda1=5,
        mu0=0.8,
        mu1=0.8,
        q01=0.1,
        q10=0.12)

tree <- tree.bisse(pars, max.taxa=50, x0=0)

The make.bisse function is a scalar function of two variables of the form loglik = loglik(c(lambda,mu))

loglik <- make.bisse(tree, tree$tip.state)

What are the MLEs?

mles <- find.mle(loglik, pars)

# these are the parmeter values that this infers:
mlepars <- mles$par

Let’s visualize the likelihood surface one parameter at a time (holding the other parameter at the MLE; not exactly profile likelihoods, but pretty close):

loglik_lambda0 <- function(lambda0) loglik(c(lambda0,mlepars[-1]))
loglik_lambda1 <- function(lambda1) loglik(c(mlepars[1],lambda1,mlepars[3:6]))

loglik_mu0 <- function(mu0) loglik(c(mlepars[1:2],mu0,mlepars[4:6]))
loglik_mu1 <- function(mu1) loglik(c(mlepars[1:3],mu1,mlepars[5:6]))

loglik_q01 <- function(q01) loglik(c(mlepars[1:4],q01,mlepars[6]))
loglik_q10 <- function(q10) loglik(c(mlepars[1:5],q10))

lambdavals <- exp( seq(-5,2,length=50))
muvals <- exp( seq(-9,2,length=50))
qvals <- exp( seq(-9,2, length=50) )

loglik_lambda0_vals <- sapply(lambdavals, loglik_lambda0)
loglik_lambda1_vals <- sapply(lambdavals, loglik_lambda1)

loglik_mu0_vals <- sapply(muvals, loglik_mu0)
loglik_mu1_vals <- sapply(muvals, loglik_mu1)

loglik_q01_vals <- sapply(qvals, loglik_q01)
loglik_q10_vals <- sapply(qvals, loglik_q10)

par(mfrow=c(3,2))

plot(lambdavals,loglik_lambda0_vals)
abline(v=pars['lambda0'],col='red')

plot(lambdavals,loglik_lambda1_vals)
abline(v=pars['lambda1'],col='red')

plot(muvals,loglik_mu0_vals)
abline(v=pars['mu0'],col='red')

plot(muvals,loglik_mu1_vals)
abline(v=pars['mu1'],col='red')

plot(qvals,loglik_q01_vals)
abline(v=pars['q01'],col='red')
plot(qvals,loglik_q10_vals)
abline(v=pars['q10'],col='red')

So, varying each parameter while holding the others at the values used to produce the simulated BiSSE tree indicate MLEs may be a good approach to estimating parameters.
The MLE differs from the “true” values used to produce the simulation, but that is expected.
Of course, these approximations of profile likelihoods don’t show how correlated some of these parameters are with each other. We might as well use MCMC to do this:
This will take quite a lot longer than our birth-death model with just 2 parameters:

mcmc_fit <- mcmc(loglik, pars, nsteps = 1000, w=1)

Let’s use an ensemble MCMC sampler.
The make.bisse function will throw errors if we try to plug in negative parameters:

loglik(-pars)

For an MCMC routine that isn’t included in diversitree, we need to ensure that we can do an unrestricted search in parameter space.

loglik_transformed <- function(pars){
    pars <- exp(pars)
    loglik <- loglik(pars)
    return(loglik)
}

loglik_transformed(log(pars))
# This looks ok.

loglik_transformed(-log(pars))
# Calculates a value without error. Good to go.

nwalkers <- 100 #ensemble size
nsteps <- 50 #number of times to update entire nsemble

# initialize the ensemble:
mcmc_inits <- matrix(nrow=nwalkers, ncol=6)
for(i in 1:nwalkers) mcmc_inits[i,] <- jitter(log(mlepars))

# run the ensemble MCMC:
mcmc_fit <- MCMCEnsemble(loglik_transformed,
    inits=mcmc_inits,
    max.iter=nwalkers*nsteps,
    n.walkers = nwalkers)

# Now, let's try to visualize the profile log-likelihoods (including
#   the curves we generated earlier):

par(mfrow=c(3,2))

plot(mcmc_fit$log.p ~ mcmc_fit$samples[,,1],xlab=bquote(lambda[0]))
#lines(log(lambdavals), loglik_lambda0_vals, col='red')

plot(mcmc_fit$log.p ~ mcmc_fit$samples[,,2],xlab=bquote(lambda[1]))
#lines(log(lambdavals), loglik_lambda1_vals, col='red')

plot(mcmc_fit$log.p ~ mcmc_fit$samples[,,3],xlab=bquote(mu[0]))
#lines(log(muvals), loglik_mu0_vals, col='red')

plot(mcmc_fit$log.p ~ mcmc_fit$samples[,,4],xlab=bquote(mu[1]))
#lines(log(muvals), loglik_mu1_vals, col='red')

plot(mcmc_fit$log.p ~ mcmc_fit$samples[,,5],xlab=bquote(q[0~1]))
#lines(log(qvals), loglik_q01_vals, col='red')

plot(mcmc_fit$log.p ~ mcmc_fit$samples[,,6],xlab=bquote(q[1~0]))
#lines(log(qvals), loglik_q10_vals, col='red')

In the vicinity of the MLE, the MCMC results should closely match the profile likelihood curves (but far away from the MLE there is no reason why they should be similar). (Why?)
Looking at the different plots, are you able to say which parameters that are more difficult to estimate than others?
What if we look at parameter correlations?

plot(mcmc_fit$samples[,,1] ~ mcmc_fit$samples[,,2],
    xlab=bquote(lambda[1]),
    ylab=bquote(lambda[0]))

    max.iter=nwalkers*nsteps,
    n.walkers = nwalkers)

# Now, let's try to visualize the profile log-likelihoods (including
#   the curves we generated earlier):

par(mfrow=c(3,2))

plot(mcmc_fit$log.p ~ mcmc_fit$samples[,,1],xlab=bquote(lambda[0]))
#lines(log(lambdavals), loglik_lambda0_vals, col='red')

plot(mcmc_fit$log.p ~ mcmc_fit$samples[,,2],xlab=bquote(lambda[1]))
#lines(log(lambdavals), loglik_lambda1_vals, col='red')

plot(mcmc_fit$log.p ~ mcmc_fit$samples[,,3],xlab=bquote(mu[0]))
#lines(log(muvals), loglik_mu0_vals, col='red')

plot(mcmc_fit$log.p ~ mcmc_fit$samples[,,4],xlab=bquote(mu[1]))
#lines(log(muvals), loglik_mu1_vals, col='red')

plot(mcmc_fit$log.p ~ mcmc_fit$samples[,,5],xlab=bquote(q[0~1]))
#lines(log(qvals), loglik_q01_vals, col='red')

plot(mcmc_fit$log.p ~ mcmc_fit$samples[,,6],xlab=bquote(q[1~0]))
#lines(log(qvals), loglik_q10_vals, col='red')

In the vicinity of the MLE, the MCMC results should closely match the profile likelihood curves (but far away from the MLE there is no reason why they should be similar). (Why?)
Looking at the different plots, are you able to say which parameters that are more difficult to estimate than others?
What if we look at parameter correlations?

plot(mcmc_fit$samples[,,1] ~ mcmc_fit$samples[,,2],
    xlab=bquote(lambda[1]),
    ylab=bquote(lambda[0]))

title: Fitting BiSSE models:
text:
1.
What parameters seem to exhibit the highest correlations? The lowest? (You will need to plot all the pairwise combinations.) How do parameter estimates compare to the values used in the simulation? Is it easier to estimate one diversification rate or the other? Ditto for the mu’s and q’s. Why do you think that is?

text:
2.
Increase the number of taxa in your tree. How does your ability to estimate parameters change? Does it become easier to estimate migration rates, or death rates? Using 200 taxa should be doable, but see how high you can go before computations get bogged down. Be sure to describe bias as well as precision of estimates.

Which migration rate is it, and why do you think it might be easier to estimate it with greater precision than the other one? It might be helpful to plot the tree.

plot(history.from.sim.discrete(tree, states=c(0,1)),
                tree, col=c('0'='black','1'='red'))

title: Fitting BiSSE models:

Set migration to be asymmetric. How easy is this to detect? You can start by assuming diversification and extinction rates are the same in each location.

Under what circumstances do you think it is possible to reliably estimate extinction rates? Carry out an analysis to confirm or refute your hypothesis.

text:

Naively, one might think that having larger differences between parameters (lambda0 vs lambda1, for example) would make estimation easier. Why is that not necessarily the case with BiSSE models? Think back to aeons ago when we simulated these models under different parameter combinations…

text:

Try setting a constraint in the log-likelihood function that mu0=mu1. Does this help estimate the other parameters? Does it make it easier to estimate the overall exctinction rate (mu)?
Hint: it works like this: loglik_constrained <- constrain(loglik, mu0 ~ mu1)

title: Fitting BiSSE models:
text:

Load the phangorn package, and use the nni function to get all of the trees that are one nni move away from your simulated tree. Evaluate the likelihood of all of the trees (but just use the MLE for the other parameters). Does the true tree carry the highest likelihood? Do you think the tree itself is identifiable? (Hint: use the chronos function to produce ultrametric trees from the nni output).

title: Simulating and fitting State-dependent speciation and extinction (SSE) models
text:
library(diversitree)
set.seed(117)

State-dependent speciation and extion (SSE) models are the subject of this set of exercises. The workhorse of these methods are the various flavors of birth-death models for generating trees.

title: Simulating and fitting State-dependent speciation and extinction (SSE) models
text: BiSSE Simulation Exercises:

title: Simulating and fitting State-dependent speciation and extinction (SSE) models
text:
1 .
Simulate a population that starts in a hostile environment but can move to a safer habitat with less predation. Assume the organisms are dumb (or just plants), so that individuals tend to spend the same amount of time in each habitat before moving to the other one. Do the simulations match your intuition about what should happen to the population in this scenario? Do you see different outcomes if migration is slow or fast relative to the birth and death rates? What happens if you simulate more taxa? (Try up to 10^4 or 10^5).
Also, try setting include.extinct=TRUE in your simulations to see how things change.

title: Simulating and fitting State-dependent speciation and extinction (SSE) models
text:
2.
Simulate a population that can move to a new habitat where there are more resources available, and organisms can produce more offspring. Assume that organisms have the same life expectancy in each area. You can also assume that individuals spend the same amount of time in each location. How does this scenario compare to the previous one, in terms of the trees you generate? Try different numbers of taxa.

title: Simulating and fitting State-dependent speciation and extinction (SSE) models
text:
3.
Now, suppose through no fault of their own that organisms are transported to a terrible environment at a much faster rate than they can escape. If you didn’t know the parameters you used, would it be obvious from the trees that one of the locations is worse than the the other?

title: Simulating and fitting State-dependent speciation and extinction (SSE) models
text:
4.
Supose the organisms are intelligent, and preferentially move to the location where there is less predation, and/or better resources. Do you think the phylogenies contain information about the crummy habitat?

title: Simulating and fitting State-dependent speciation and extinction (SSE) models
text:
5.
Try to simulate multi-state models using tree.musse. This example comes from the help page for ?tree.musse. Modify this to include 4 states with equal rates of movement between all of the states.

## A 3-state example where movement is only allowed between neighbouring
## states (1 <-> 2 <-> 3), and where speciation and extinction rates
## increase moving from 1 -> 2 -> 3:

pars <- c(.1,  .15,  .2,  # lambda 1, 2, 3
          .03, .045, .06, # mu 1, 2, 3
          .05, 0,         # q12, q13
          .05, .05,       # q21, q23
          0,   .05)       # q31, q32

set.seed(2)
phy <- tree.musse(pars, 30, x0=1, include.extinct=TRUE)

h <- history.from.sim.discrete(phy, 1:3)
plot(h, phy, cex=.7)








Modify this to include 4 states with equal rates of movement between all of the states.

title: Simulating and fitting State-dependent speciation and extinction (SSE) models
text:
Example: Benchmarking from simulations
Simulate a BiSSE tree:

pars <- c(
        lambda0=1,
        lambda1=5,
        mu0=0.8,
        mu1=0.8,
        q01=0.1,
        q10=0.12)

set.seed(117)
tree <- tree.bisse(pars, max.taxa=50, x0=0)

The make.bisse function is a scalar function of two variables of the form loglik = loglik(c(lambda,mu))

loglik <- make.bisse(tree, tree$tip.state)

What are the MLEs?

mles <- find.mle(loglik, pars)

# these are the parmeter values that this infers:
print(mlepars <- mles$par)

     lambda0      lambda1          mu0          mu1          q01          q10 
5.170918e-01 3.708414e+00 3.894023e-09 8.022539e-01 3.424501e-01 5.801057e-02 


Let’s visualize the likelihood surface one parameter at a time (holding the other parameter at the MLE; not exactly profile likelihoods, but pretty close):

loglik_lambda0 <- function(lambda0) loglik(c(lambda0,mlepars[-1]))
loglik_lambda1 <- function(lambda1) loglik(c(mlepars[1],lambda1,mlepars[3:6]))

loglik_mu0 <- function(mu0) loglik(c(mlepars[1:2],mu0,mlepars[4:6]))
loglik_mu1 <- function(mu1) loglik(c(mlepars[1:3],mu1,mlepars[5:6]))

loglik_q01 <- function(q01) loglik(c(mlepars[1:4],q01,mlepars[6]))
loglik_q10 <- function(q10) loglik(c(mlepars[1:5],q10))

lambdavals <- exp( seq(-5,2,length=50))
muvals <- exp( seq(-9,2,length=50))
qvals <- exp( seq(-9,2, length=50) )

loglik_lambda0_vals <- sapply(lambdavals, loglik_lambda0)
loglik_lambda1_vals <- sapply(lambdavals, loglik_lambda1)

loglik_mu0_vals <- sapply(muvals, loglik_mu0)
loglik_mu1_vals <- sapply(muvals, loglik_mu1)

loglik_q01_vals <- sapply(qvals, loglik_q01)
loglik_q10_vals <- sapply(qvals, loglik_q10)

par(mfrow=c(3,2))

plot(lambdavals,loglik_lambda0_vals)
abline(v=pars['lambda0'],col='red')

plot(lambdavals,loglik_lambda1_vals)
abline(v=pars['lambda1'],col='red')

plot(muvals,loglik_mu0_vals)
abline(v=pars['mu0'],col='red')

plot(muvals,loglik_mu1_vals)
abline(v=pars['mu1'],col='red')

plot(qvals,loglik_q01_vals)
abline(v=pars['q01'],col='red')
plot(qvals,loglik_q10_vals)
abline(v=pars['q10'],col='red')








So, the parameters might be identifiable, but already this looks more challenging than the birth-death models we fit earlier today.
The MLE differs from the “true” values used to produce the simulation, but that is expected.
Of course, these approximations of profile likelihoods don’t show how correlated some of these parameters are with each other. We might as well use MCMC to do this:
This will take quite a lot longer than our birth-death model with just 2 parameters:

mcmc_fit <- mcmc(loglik, pars, nsteps = 1000, w=1)

Looking at the different plots of the MCMC output, are you able to say which parameters that are more difficult to estimate than others?

title: Simulating Multi-type birth-death models in R
text:
library(TreeSim)

We will examine the multi-type birth death model without migration. Individuals in location i can give birth to individuals in any state j, but lineages do not move between locations otherwise.

n<-200
lambda <- rbind(c(.05,1),c(1,.2))
mu <- c(1,1)
sampprob <-c(0.1,0.1)
numbsim<-1
trees<-lapply(rep(n,numbsim),sim.bdtypes.stt.taxa,
    lambdavector=lambda,deathvector=mu,sampprobvector=sampprob)

tree <- trees[[1]]
plot(tree, show.tip.label=F)
tiplabels(pch=21,bg=c('blue','red')[tree$states])

title: Simulating Multi-type birth-death models in R
text:
1.
Suppose that we want to use this as a model for phylogeography. The sim.bdtypes.stt.taxa function does not model migrations per se, but rather births from state i to state j. If migration events are rare relative to replication, then maybe this is a reasonable framework. Use ace(tree$state,tree) with any constraints on the Markov process’s Q matrix that you like, and plot the reconstructed ancestral states on the tree (unfortuantely, sim.bdtypes.stt.taxa does not give information about states along edges, or at internal nodes).

title: Simulating Multi-type birth-death models in R
text:
2.
If the matrix \(\Lambda\) has large off-diagonal terms, what do you see in your simulated trees? Could this be used to model anything biologically interesting?

If the matrix \(\Lambda\) is diagonal-dominant but one of the \(\lambda_{ii}\) is much larger than the rest, what do you notice about the simulated trees? Comment on the ability to estimate parameters of models in this case.

title: Fitting Markov models on trees using the ancestral character estimation (ace) function in R
text:
# rm(list=ls())

library(ape)
library(expm) # matrix exponential
library(ggtree)
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)
library(cowplot)
library(scatterpie)

Ancestral character estimation methods can be based on a variety of approaches, but we will consider Markov chains superimposed on phylogenies. In the accompanying code to this we will learn how to fit these models using Maximum Likelihood.


Our working example will be this Seasonal Coronavirus NL63 phylogeny obtained from NextStrain: https://nextstrain.org/seasonal-cov/nl63
In the left-hand panel on the webpage at NextStrain, you can see the option to Color in the phylogeny according to a variety of characteristics, including “Genotype”. If you select Genotype and then select “Spike” as the gene, and then examine position 100, you will see something fascinating: the phylogeny displays clear evidence of mutations from the amino acid S (Glycine) to P (Aspartic acid), and vice versa. The phylogeny on NextStrain colors in the internal edges of their phylogeny to display where the mutations may have occurred. Our goal is to try to replicate this using the ace function in R.
As a side note: Seasonal Coronavirus NL63 is fascinating because it uses the same ACE2 receptor to enter cells as SARS-CoV-2. In both viruses, the Spike protein on the virus is a little gizmo that helps the viruses bind to ACE2 and enter cells during infection. You have probably experienced NL63 infection multiple times in your life up to this point – usually in the form of a cold, or completely asymptomatic.
Let’s see how these amino acids in the Spike protein evolved over time. First, let’s read in the tree and plot it. I have manually provided tip data below to reproduce what we see on NextStrain.

tree <- read.tree('coronavirus_nl63/nextstrain_seasonal-cov_nl63_timetree.nwk')

# Root the tree (not the most technically appropriate way to do this, but good enough for our illustrative purposes):
tree <- root(tree, node=Ntip(tree)+1 )

# Make the tree fully bifurcating:
tree <- multi2di(tree)

tree$edge.length[tree$edge.length < 0] <- .01
tree$edge.length[tree$edge.length == 0] <- .01


# Some of these tips have an amino acid change in the gene for the Spike protein. From Nextstrain, we can 
# see that it is these ones:

Stips <- tree$tip.label
Ntips <- setdiff(extract.clade(tree, 402)$tip.label, extract.clade(tree, 473)$tip.label)
Stips <- setdiff(tree$tip.label, Ntips)

x <- rep(0, Ntip(tree))
names(x) <- tree$tip.label
x[Stips] <- "S"
x[Ntips] <- "P"

p <- ggtree(tree) + ggtitle('Seasonal Coronavirus NL63') +
    theme(plot.title=element_text(hjust=0.5, size=18, face="bold"))

p <- p %<+% data.frame(label=tree$tip.label, aminoacid = as.factor(x))

p + geom_tippoint(aes(col=aminoacid))








What do we think the evolutionary history was? There are a few possibilities I can readily envision:
Hypothesis 1: The original NL63 virus had amino acid S, and then mutated into P in the bottom clade, and then there was a back-mutation from P to S.
Hypothesis 2: Originally the amino acid was P, and there have been two mutations from P to S – one of these was long ago (and produced the large clade on top), and one of the mutations was more recent (the small blue clade nested within the larger red “P” clade).
Hypothesis 3: The original amino acid was S, but there have been no back-mutations from P to S; rather, there have been at least four instances of mutation from S to P in the bottom clade.
You can probably come up with additional hypotheses besides these. At this stage what do you think is the most likely scenario?



To determine what the most likely evolutionary scenario was, we can use a mathematical model that describes change in the trait over time using a Markov chain with two states (corresponding to P and S). There are two versions of this model:
-Model 1: the rate of changing from P to S equals the rate of change from S to P (equal rates model, “ER”)
-Model 2: the rate from P to S can be different from the rate from S to P (all rates different model, “ARD”)
Let’s fit both of these models to the data using ace:

fit1 <- ace(x, tree, type='discrete', model='ER')

fit2 <- ace(x, tree, type='discrete', model='ARD')

The type=‘discrete’ argument tells ace that our data at the tips (x) correspond to states of a discrete state-space Markov chain. The ace function can handle continuous data as well, but we will not make use of this.
We can examine the parameter estimates in each case:

fit1


    Ancestral Character Estimation

Call: ace(x = x, phy = tree, type = "discrete", model = "ER")

    Log-likelihood: -11.89298 

Rate index matrix:
  P S
P . 1
S 1 .

Parameter estimates:
 rate index estimate std-err
          1    9e-04   5e-04

Scaled likelihoods at the root (type '...$lik.anc' to get them for all nodes):
        P         S 
0.2746174 0.7253826 


The output above tells us what the structure of our Markov model is (Rate index matrix), as well as the value of the objective function (Log-likelihood) at the Maximum Likelihood paramter estimate (Parameter estimates). There is only one parameter estimated because we used an “equal rates” model. The estimate for the rate is a pretty small number, which makes perfect sense – while there is some evidence for a back mutation (from P to S), or possibly for \(\sim 4\) mutations from S to P, there seems to be a fairly small number of mutations overall. The rate estimate we are getting seems to make sense.
The last bit of the output is particularly salient for our question we began with: it tell us the “Scaled likelihoods at the root”, and assigns a higher value to S than to P. This means that, under this model, the ancestral virus most likely had the amino acid S rather than the amino acid P. This already tells us that, at least to the extent we think our Markov model is reasonable, the data seem more consistent with Hypotheses 1 or 3 than Hypothesis 2.
Okay, so far so good - but maybe I think the equal rates model is unreasonable to begin with. After all, it is a little bit striking that there is a huge clade in the phylogeny with no evidence of mutation from S to P whatsoever, but there does seem to be evidence of back-mutation from P to S. Maybe the rate of mutation from P to S is much faster than the rate from S to P. This is what our second model allows for. Let’s see how those estimates turned out:

fit2


    Ancestral Character Estimation

Call: ace(x = x, phy = tree, type = "discrete", model = "ARD")

    Log-likelihood: -10.28796 

Rate index matrix:
  P S
P . 2
S 1 .

Parameter estimates:
 rate index estimate std-err
          1   0.0000  0.0017
          2   0.0036  0.0033

Scaled likelihoods at the root (type '...$lik.anc' to get them for all nodes):
P S 
1 0 


Oh my! That is rather striking! In this model we are estimating two rates (S to P, and P to S). The Maximum Likelihood Estimate (MLE) says that the rate from S to P equals zero. Nada. Zilch. The rate of change from P to S is 0.0036, which is quite a lot larger than the rate we estimated in Model 1. The Scaled likelihoods at the root say that the ancestor had amino acid P with certainty. So, if we think Model 2 is a reasonable model, then Hypothesis 2 is the best explanation for the evolutionary history. Under the model, the best explanation for the data is that the original virus had amino acid P, and there have been two mutations from P to S down to the present day.
In response to an estimated rate equal to zero, my knee-jerk reaction is to look at the standard errors (“std-err”) in the parameter estimates. Sometimes, numerical issues might result in weird behavior near the boundary of parameter space (rates are always non-negative, so zero is such a boundary in our case). The estimate for rate 1 has a standard error of 0.0017. If there had been some kind of numerical strangeness, we might see NA and a warning pop up. That didn’t happen here, so the MLE of 0.0000 for rate 1 appears to be genuine. It is also worth noting that the scaled likelihoods at the root are calculated under the MLE, and do not necessarily account for uncertainty in the MLE (as quantified by the standard errors). So, while our best estimate for the evolutionary history has P as the overall ancestor, a nonzero estimate for rate 1 might allow for a small possibility that the ancestral virus had S. That is, in the same way that we have a confidence set for our rate parameters, there is an implied confidence set of evolutionary histories, some of which have S at the root of the tree – but the scaled likelihoods at the root do not reflect this.



So far, all we have done is fit our models and interpret the parameter estimates. However, we can do a little bit more than this. Similar to NextStrain, we can annotate our tree with our estimated models to indicate what the ancestral states may have been. This is, after all, the point of ancestral state inference/ancestral character estimation.
The scaled likelihoods a the root that we see in the printout from fit1 and fit2 are built up from likelihoods at all of the internal nodes desending from the root. These are tucked away in the fitted model output (“lik.anc”):

head(fit1$lik.anc)

            P            S
401 0.2746174 7.253826e-01
402 0.9810853 1.891467e-02
403 0.9998225 1.775022e-04
404 1.0000000 1.573183e-10
405 1.0000000 5.274620e-13
406 1.0000000 3.038069e-12


This is a datarame with the scaled likelihoods of the two states for each node. The tree has 400 tips, so the nodes are numbered 401 through 799 (a bifurcating tree with N tips has N-1 internal nodes). Each row in the dataframe above is one of the nodes, and the values in the columns are the relative likelihoods that it resides in state P or state S under the MLE. We can use this data to plot estimated states at the internal nodes.

# Let's add in node labels for the tree:
tree$node.label <- (Ntip(tree)+1):(Ntip(tree) + tree$Nnode)

# what were the most likely node states?
nodestates1 <- c('P','S')[apply(fit1$lik.anc, 1, function(x) which(x==max(x)) )]

treedat1 <- data.frame(label=tree$tip.label, aminoacid = as.factor(x))
nodedat1 <- data.frame(label=as.character((Ntip(tree)+1):(Ntip(tree)+tree$Nnode)), aminoacid=nodestates1)

treedat1 <- rbind(treedat1, nodedat1)

p1 <- ggtree(tree) + ggtitle('ER model') +
    theme(plot.title=element_text(hjust=0.5, size=18, face="bold"))

p1 <- p1 %<+% treedat1

p1 <- p1 + geom_tippoint(aes(col=aminoacid)) + geom_nodepoint(aes(col=aminoacid))  + 
    theme(legend.position='none')

# Now, the all-rates different model:

# what were the most likely node states?
nodestates2 <- c('P','S')[apply(fit2$lik.anc, 1, function(x) which(x==max(x)) )]

treedat2 <- data.frame(label=tree$tip.label, aminoacid = as.factor(x))
nodedat2 <- data.frame(label=as.character((Ntip(tree)+1):(Ntip(tree)+tree$Nnode)), aminoacid=nodestates2)

treedat2 <- rbind(treedat2, nodedat2)

p2 <- ggtree(tree) + ggtitle('ARD model') +
    theme(plot.title=element_text(hjust=0.5, size=18, face="bold"))

p2 <- p2 %<+% treedat2

p2 <- p2 + geom_tippoint(aes(col=aminoacid)) + geom_nodepoint(aes(col=aminoacid)) 

legend <- get_legend(p2 + theme(legend.box.margin=margin(0,0,0,12)))
plot_grid(p1,p2+theme(legend.position='none'),legend, nrow=1, rel_widths=c(1,1,.3))








Aside from the differences at the root of the tree, the two models appear to mostly agree with each other in terms of the ancestral states of most of the other ancestors. However, the similarity in ancestral reconstructions belies the very different interpretations of the evolutionary histories between the models.

In the ER model, mutations are possible in both directions under the MLE
In the ARD model, mutations from S to P never occur under the MLE
The ARD model makes the prediction that, given enough time, every virus we sample will have the amino acid S, and P will go extinct.




How do we decide which of these is more plausible? One way to try to address this is with a Generalized Likelihood Ratio test. The ER model (one estimated parameter in our example here) is a special case of the ARD model (two estimated parameters in our example here). Another way to put it is that the ER model is “nested” within the ARD model. This is precisely the condition under which we may apply the GLR test.
To carry out a GLR test, we calculate a test statistic obtained from the ratio of the two likelihoods, hence the name. Let’s look at the log-likelihoods for our two models:

fit1$loglik

[1] -11.89298

fit2$loglik

[1] -10.28796


You wil notice that the log-likelihood for the ARD model is larger than the log-likelihood for the ER model. This indicates that the ARD model fits the data better than the ER model. This makes perfect sense, because a model with more parameters should fit the data better than a model with fewer paramters.
The question is whether the improvement in model fit is meaningful, or whether we are overfitting to noise in the data.
The idea behind the GLR test is: if the the difference in log-likelihoods (or equivalently, the logarithm of the ratio of the likelihoods) is huge, then the improvement from the more complicated model is meaningful, i.e. not overfit. A small improvement in the fit of the model, as quantified by a marginally higher log-likelihood, indicates that we are adding more paramters/complexity to our model without producing a dramatically improved fit.
From statistics, theory establishes that, provided we have a sufficiently large dataset, the quantity
\(\lambda_{LR} = -2 (l_{\text{ER}} - l_{\text{ARD}})\)
is approximately distributed according to a \(\chi^2\) distribution under the null hypothesis that data arise from the simpler model. The degrees of freedom for this \(\chi^2\) distribution is equal to the difference in the number of parameters between the models (so in our case, a single degree of freedom). Values of \(\lambda_{LR}\) that are huge are evidence against the null hypothesis. If we want to control type 1 errors (false-positives) at \(5\%\), we may say that values of \(\lambda_{LR}\) landing above the \(95\%\)-ile of the distribution lead us to reject the null hypothesis.
Recall that a \(p\)-value is the probability of observing a value of the test statistic as extreme or more extreme (under the null hypothesis) than the one we calculate from the data.
So, to carry out a GLR test, we proceed as follows:

lambda <- -2 * (fit1$loglik - fit2$loglik)
lambda

[1] 3.21004

# we have one degree of freedom because the more complicated model has one more paramter than the simpler model:
dfs <- 1

# calculate the p-value, which is the probability of obseving a lambda as extreme or more extreme than the one we calculated above; 
# chisq variables are always non-negative, and "extreme" in this case means large and positive -- so we want the upper tail probability,
# rather than the cumulative probability:

p <- pchisq(lambda, df=dfs, lower.tail=F) 

p

[1] 0.07318771


So, we have a p-value equal to 0.073. Most people would say that there is not sufficient evidence to reject the null hypothesis (ER model) in favor of the alternative (ARD model) based on this.
In other words, there is not strong evidence to support unidirectional change in amino acids.
All of these conclusions are conditional on the Markov models we are using. Remember: junk in = junk out.
These conclusions are totally contingent upon the confidence we do (or do not have) in these models.
There are some potential pitfalls of these models that we should articulate now:

Either of these amino acids could carry a fitness benefit that could lead to increased numbers of infections by one lineage or the other. We are not modeling this effect
The models are assuming that rates are constant in time. This may not be a reasonable assumption to make.

Can you think of any other criticisms?
From the methodological point of view there is another thing we have not done: we have not obtained an estimate for when mutations actually occurred. Our estimates of ancestral states should inform the timing and number of mutations, but more work is needed to do so.
In a later section (stochastic character mapping), we will do both of these things.

title: Fitting Markov models on trees using the ancestral character estimation (ace) function in R
text:
Our working example will be this Seasonal Coronavirus NL63 phylogeny obtained from NextStrain: https://nextstrain.org/seasonal-cov/nl63
In the left-hand panel on the webpage at NextStrain, you can see the option to Color in the phylogeny according to a variety of characteristics, including “Genotype”. If you select Genotype and then select “Spike” as the gene, and then examine position 100, you will see something fascinating: the phylogeny displays clear evidence of mutations from the amino acid S (Glycine) to P (Aspartic acid), and vice versa. The phylogeny on NextStrain colors in the internal edges of their phylogeny to display where the mutations may have occurred. Our goal is to try to replicate this using the ace function in R.
As a side note: Seasonal Coronavirus NL63 is fascinating because it uses the same ACE2 receptor to enter cells as SARS-CoV-2. In both viruses, the Spike protein on the virus is a little gizmo that helps the viruses bind to ACE2 and enter cells during infection. You have probably experienced NL63 infection multiple times in your life up to this point – usually in the form of a cold, or completely asymptomatic.
Let’s see how these amino acids in the Spike protein evolved over time. First, let’s read in the tree and plot it. I have manually provided tip data below to reproduce what we see on NextStrain.

tree <- read.tree('coronavirus_nl63/nextstrain_seasonal-cov_nl63_timetree.nwk')

# Root the tree (not the most technically appropriate way to do this, but good enough for our illustrative purposes):
tree <- root(tree, node=Ntip(tree)+1 )

# Make the tree fully bifurcating:
tree <- multi2di(tree)

tree$edge.length[tree$edge.length < 0] <- .01
tree$edge.length[tree$edge.length == 0] <- .01


# Some of these tips have an amino acid change in the gene for the Spike protein. From Nextstrain, we can 
# see that it is these ones:

Stips <- tree$tip.label
Ntips <- setdiff(extract.clade(tree, 402)$tip.label, extract.clade(tree, 473)$tip.label)
Stips <- setdiff(tree$tip.label, Ntips)

x <- rep(0, Ntip(tree))
names(x) <- tree$tip.label
x[Stips] <- "S"
x[Ntips] <- "P"

p <- ggtree(tree) + ggtitle('Seasonal Coronavirus NL63') +
    theme(plot.title=element_text(hjust=0.5, size=18, face="bold"))

p <- p %<+% data.frame(label=tree$tip.label, aminoacid = as.factor(x))

p + geom_tippoint(aes(col=aminoacid))








What do we think the evolutionary history was? There are a few possibilities I can readily envision:
Hypothesis 1: The original NL63 virus had amino acid S, and then mutated into P in the bottom clade, and then there was a back-mutation from P to S.
Hypothesis 2: Originally the amino acid was P, and there have been two mutations from P to S – one of these was long ago (and produced the large clade on top), and one of the mutations was more recent (the small blue clade nested within the larger red “P” clade).
Hypothesis 3: The original amino acid was S, but there have been no back-mutations from P to S; rather, there have been at least four instances of mutation from S to P in the bottom clade.
You can probably come up with additional hypotheses besides these. At this stage what do you think is the most likely scenario?

title: Fitting Markov models on trees using the ancestral character estimation (ace) function in R
text:
To determine what the most likely evolutionary scenario was, we can use a mathematical model that describes change in the trait over time using a Markov chain with two states (corresponding to P and S). There are two versions of this model:
-Model 1: the rate of changing from P to S equals the rate of change from S to P (equal rates model, “ER”)
-Model 2: the rate from P to S can be different from the rate from S to P (all rates different model, “ARD”)
Let’s fit both of these models to the data using ace:

fit1 <- ace(x, tree, type='discrete', model='ER')

fit2 <- ace(x, tree, type='discrete', model='ARD')

The type=‘discrete’ argument tells ace that our data at the tips (x) correspond to states of a discrete state-space Markov chain. The ace function can handle continuous data as well, but we will not make use of this.
We can examine the parameter estimates in each case:

fit1


    Ancestral Character Estimation

Call: ace(x = x, phy = tree, type = "discrete", model = "ER")

    Log-likelihood: -11.89298 

Rate index matrix:
  P S
P . 1
S 1 .

Parameter estimates:
 rate index estimate std-err
          1    9e-04   5e-04

Scaled likelihoods at the root (type '...$lik.anc' to get them for all nodes):
        P         S 
0.2746174 0.7253826 


The output above tells us what the structure of our Markov model is (Rate index matrix), as well as the value of the objective function (Log-likelihood) at the Maximum Likelihood paramter estimate (Parameter estimates). There is only one parameter estimated because we used an “equal rates” model. The estimate for the rate is a pretty small number, which makes perfect sense – while there is some evidence for a back mutation (from P to S), or possibly for \(\sim 4\) mutations from S to P, there seems to be a fairly small number of mutations overall. The rate estimate we are getting seems to make sense.
The last bit of the output is particularly salient for our question we began with: it tell us the “Scaled likelihoods at the root”, and assigns a higher value to S than to P. This means that, under this model, the ancestral virus most likely had the amino acid S rather than the amino acid P. This already tells us that, at least to the extent we think our Markov model is reasonable, the data seem more consistent with Hypotheses 1 or 3 than Hypothesis 2.
Okay, so far so good - but maybe I think the equal rates model is unreasonable to begin with. After all, it is a little bit striking that there is a huge clade in the phylogeny with no evidence of mutation from S to P whatsoever, but there does seem to be evidence of back-mutation from P to S. Maybe the rate of mutation from P to S is much faster than the rate from S to P. This is what our second model allows for. Let’s see how those estimates turned out:

fit2


    Ancestral Character Estimation

Call: ace(x = x, phy = tree, type = "discrete", model = "ARD")

    Log-likelihood: -10.28796 

Rate index matrix:
  P S
P . 2
S 1 .

Parameter estimates:
 rate index estimate std-err
          1   0.0000  0.0017
          2   0.0036  0.0033

Scaled likelihoods at the root (type '...$lik.anc' to get them for all nodes):
P S 
1 0 


Oh my! That is rather striking! In this model we are estimating two rates (S to P, and P to S). The Maximum Likelihood Estimate (MLE) says that the rate from S to P equals zero. Nada. Zilch. The rate of change from P to S is 0.0036, which is quite a lot larger than the rate we estimated in Model 1. The Scaled likelihoods at the root say that the ancestor had amino acid P with certainty. So, if we think Model 2 is a reasonable model, then Hypothesis 2 is the best explanation for the evolutionary history. Under the model, the best explanation for the data is that the original virus had amino acid P, and there have been two mutations from P to S down to the present day.
In response to an estimated rate equal to zero, my knee-jerk reaction is to look at the standard errors (“std-err”) in the parameter estimates. Sometimes, numerical issues might result in weird behavior near the boundary of parameter space (rates are always non-negative, so zero is such a boundary in our case). The estimate for rate 1 has a standard error of 0.0017. If there had been some kind of numerical strangeness, we might see NA and a warning pop up. That didn’t happen here, so the MLE of 0.0000 for rate 1 appears to be genuine. It is also worth noting that the scaled likelihoods at the root are calculated under the MLE, and do not necessarily account for uncertainty in the MLE (as quantified by the standard errors). So, while our best estimate for the evolutionary history has P as the overall ancestor, a nonzero estimate for rate 1 might allow for a small possibility that the ancestral virus had S. That is, in the same way that we have a confidence set for our rate parameters, there is an implied confidence set of evolutionary histories, some of which have S at the root of the tree – but the scaled likelihoods at the root do not reflect this.

title: Fitting Markov models on trees using the ancestral character estimation (ace) function in R
text:
So far, all we have done is fit our models and interpret the parameter estimates. However, we can do a little bit more than this. Similar to NextStrain, we can annotate our tree with our estimated models to indicate what the ancestral states may have been. This is, after all, the point of ancestral state inference/ancestral character estimation.
The scaled likelihoods a the root that we see in the printout from fit1 and fit2 are built up from likelihoods at all of the internal nodes desending from the root. These are tucked away in the fitted model output (“lik.anc”):

head(fit1$lik.anc)

            P            S
401 0.2746174 7.253826e-01
402 0.9810853 1.891467e-02
403 0.9998225 1.775022e-04
404 1.0000000 1.573183e-10
405 1.0000000 5.274620e-13
406 1.0000000 3.038069e-12


This is a datarame with the scaled likelihoods of the two states for each node. The tree has 400 tips, so the nodes are numbered 401 through 799 (a bifurcating tree with N tips has N-1 internal nodes). Each row in the dataframe above is one of the nodes, and the values in the columns are the relative likelihoods that it resides in state P or state S under the MLE. We can use this data to plot estimated states at the internal nodes.

# Let's add in node labels for the tree:
tree$node.label <- (Ntip(tree)+1):(Ntip(tree) + tree$Nnode)

# what were the most likely node states?
nodestates1 <- c('P','S')[apply(fit1$lik.anc, 1, function(x) which(x==max(x)) )]

treedat1 <- data.frame(label=tree$tip.label, aminoacid = as.factor(x))
nodedat1 <- data.frame(label=as.character((Ntip(tree)+1):(Ntip(tree)+tree$Nnode)), aminoacid=nodestates1)

treedat1 <- rbind(treedat1, nodedat1)

p1 <- ggtree(tree) + ggtitle('ER model') +
    theme(plot.title=element_text(hjust=0.5, size=18, face="bold"))

p1 <- p1 %<+% treedat1

p1 <- p1 + geom_tippoint(aes(col=aminoacid)) + geom_nodepoint(aes(col=aminoacid))  + 
    theme(legend.position='none')

# Now, the all-rates different model:

# what were the most likely node states?
nodestates2 <- c('P','S')[apply(fit2$lik.anc, 1, function(x) which(x==max(x)) )]

treedat2 <- data.frame(label=tree$tip.label, aminoacid = as.factor(x))
nodedat2 <- data.frame(label=as.character((Ntip(tree)+1):(Ntip(tree)+tree$Nnode)), aminoacid=nodestates2)

treedat2 <- rbind(treedat2, nodedat2)

p2 <- ggtree(tree) + ggtitle('ARD model') +
    theme(plot.title=element_text(hjust=0.5, size=18, face="bold"))

p2 <- p2 %<+% treedat2

p2 <- p2 + geom_tippoint(aes(col=aminoacid)) + geom_nodepoint(aes(col=aminoacid)) 

legend <- get_legend(p2 + theme(legend.box.margin=margin(0,0,0,12)))
plot_grid(p1,p2+theme(legend.position='none'),legend, nrow=1, rel_widths=c(1,1,.3))








Aside from the differences at the root of the tree, the two models appear to mostly agree with each other in terms of the ancestral states of most of the other ancestors. However, the similarity in ancestral reconstructions belies the very different interpretations of the evolutionary histories between the models.

In the ER model, mutations are possible in both directions under the MLE
In the ARD model, mutations from S to P never occur under the MLE
The ARD model makes the prediction that, given enough time, every virus we sample will have the amino acid S, and P will go extinct.

title: Simulating event times
text:
An essential aspect of stochastic character mapping is simulating events in Markov chains. We have seen that we can calculate transition probabilities along branches of a phylogeny, and that these can be used to determine the the probability of particular configurations of states at the internal nodes and tips of a tree. Armed with a Markov model and the transition probabilities that can be calculated from its generator, we can simulate configurations of states at internal nodes.





The strategy for stochastic character mapping relies on simulating changes of state for Markov chains. So far, we have seen how to calculate transition probabilities given a fixed amount of time.
To illustrate this idea, let’s begin with a simple 2-state Markov chain.

Q <- matrix(c(-1,100,1,-100), nrow=2, byrow=T)

This generator says that if we are in state 1, we move to state 2 at a rate equal to 1. If we are in state 2, we move back to state 1 at a rate equal to 100. So, we expect to be spending more time in state 1 than in state 2. In fact, the relative amount of time we expect to reside in either state is given by the stationary distribution
\(\pi = (\frac{100}{101},\frac{1}{101})^T\).
The output of this simulation is going to be a list/vector/whatever of event times. We start at time zero. I’m going to decide at the outset that I want to simulate 50 changes of state, so we will have 50 event times.

n <- 50

events <- rep(0, n)
states <- rep(1,n)

We have to decide whether to start in state 1 or state 2. I have declared that we start in state 1 above.
Because the rates of our stochastic rate matrix Q are constant in time, the time until each event is described by an exponential distribution. The procedure we are about to implement is known as Gillespie’s Algorithm. The process we are about to simulate is also a type of Inhomogeneous Poisson Process.
The algorithm proceeds as follows. If we are currently in state 1, we know that we switch to state 2 at rate \(q_{21}\). In the stochastic setting, the amount of time until the state change is an Exponentially-distributed random variable with rate \(q_{21}\), so that \(T_{21} \sim q_{21} e^{-q_{21}t}\). If we are currently in state 2, the time until a transition to state 1 follows \(T_{12} \sim q_{12} e^{-q_{12}t}\).



Which convention are we using for \(q_{ij}\)? Is this the transition rate from state \(i\) to state \(j\), or the rate into state \(i\) from state \(j\)?
Let’s proceed to simulate this Markov chain in continuous time:

for(i in 2:n){
    state=states[i-1]
    events[i] <- events[i-1] + rexp(1, rate= -Q[state, state])

    states[i] <- if(state==1){2}else{1}
}

dat <- cbind(events, states)
plot(states~events,dat,type='s',xlab='Time',ylab='State')








There are a couple of takeaways from this plot. First, we see that the system is switching between states 1 and 2 at random times. Second, the system appears to be spending more time in state 1 than in state 2. Let’s verify that this matches the stationary distribution.

# What was the total simulation time?
Ttot <- tail(dat,n=1)[1]

# Calculate the amounts of time spent in each state:

diffdat <- cbind(diff(dat[,'events']),dat[-dim(dat)[1],'states']) 

# How much total time in state 1?
T1 <-  sum(diffdat[diffdat[,2]==1,1])

# How much total time in state 2?
T2 <-  sum(diffdat[diffdat[,2]==2,1])

print(c(T1/Ttot, T2/Ttot))

[1] 0.990029182 0.009970818


The fraction of time spent in state 2 should be 1/101 \(\approx 0.0099\). We are simulating over a fairly short chunk of time, so we expect some randomness in any given simulation. If we simulate for a longer period of time, these time averages should approach the stationary distribution. Try changing n above to a large number (like 10000) to confirm this.



Modify the event time simulation above to run for a pre-specified amount of time (or branch length) and an undetermined number of events. Simulate realizations of the stochastic process that accomodate predefined initial and final states, or “boundary data”. (Hint: you may include a step that invovles rejecting a trajectory if it does not match with your boundary data.)



In simulating a stochastic character mapping, does it matter which direction we simulate from? Mathematically, there is no reason why we couldn’t use tip states as initial conditions, but that doesn’t really seem consistent with evolution occurring in forward time. Are there situations where the two approaches are equivalent? Why or why not?

title: Simulating stochasting character mappings
text:
# rm(list=ls())

library(ape)
library(expm) # matrix exponential
library(ggtree)
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)
library(cowplot)
library(scatterpie)
library(phytools)

Carrying on from our last example of Seasonal Coronavirus NL63, we seek to address the following questions:


How often did the S amino acid mutate into P, and vice versa?


Where precisely do we think mutations occurred on the phylogeny?


With ancestral character estimation (ace), we approached answers to these questions. We considered two types of Markov models (equal rates, ER, and all rates different, ARD). The MLEs of the model parameters carry pretty different interpretations, but the ancestral estimates of past ancestors are largely insensitive to the particular model we used. The main difference we saw was concerning the inferred root state of the phylogeny in each model.
Stochastic character mapping, or character mapping, uses our fitted Markov chain models to annotate phylogenies with mutations along edges. So far, we have just been annotating internal nodes of the phylogenies with their most likely states, given the models and the data (tip data + phylogeny).
To perform character mapping, we will make use of a suite of functions from the phytools package in R.
Let’s start by loading in our Coronavirus NL63 phylogeny:

tree <- read.tree('coronavirus_nl63/nextstrain_seasonal-cov_nl63_timetree.nwk')

# Root the tree (not the most technically appropriate way to do this, but good enough for our illustrative purposes):
tree <- root(tree, node=Ntip(tree)+1 )

# Make the tree fully bifurcating:
tree <- multi2di(tree)

tree$edge.length[tree$edge.length < 0] <- .01
tree$edge.length[tree$edge.length == 0] <- .01


# Some of these tips have an amino acid change in the gene for the Spike protein. From Nextstrain, we can 
# see that it is these ones:

Stips <- tree$tip.label
Ntips <- setdiff(extract.clade(tree, 402)$tip.label, extract.clade(tree, 473)$tip.label)
Stips <- setdiff(tree$tip.label, Ntips)

x <- rep(0, Ntip(tree))
names(x) <- tree$tip.label
x[Stips] <- "S"
x[Ntips] <- "P"

p <- ggtree(tree) + ggtitle('Seasonal Coronavirus NL63') +
    theme(plot.title=element_text(hjust=0.5, size=18, face="bold"))

p <- p %<+% data.frame(label=tree$tip.label, aminoacid = as.factor(x))

p + geom_tippoint(aes(col=aminoacid))








Stochastic character mapping involves 3 steps:


Fit a Markov model to the phylogeny (we did this using ace already)


Sample ancestral state at internal nodes using the scaled likelihoods (calculated in step 1)


Use the estimates from the fitted model to simulate changes of state along edges of the phylogeny that are compatible with the node states at the two ends of each edge (similar to how we simulated event times in the previous tutorial)


That’s it. Conceptually, it’s super simple. Computationally, it can be a little bit intense to simulate changes of state along edges that are compatible with node states at either end of the edge.
The phytools package has a few functions that we will use.
One of these is fitMk. This function is similar to ace, except that user may supply a “root prior” (e.g. 50% probability that the root is in state S or P, for our example). When we supply a root prior, we are optimizing a different objective function than ace. By default, fitMk uses a “flat” root prior, similar to what the likelihood function for ace does. The second distinction between fitMk and ace is that they use slightly different optimizers to find Maximum Likelihood Estimates (MLEs). Sometimes it can be convenient to try using both functions if one of them encounters numerical problems during optimization. It can also be a good idea to obtain estimates from both methods as a sanity check (although estimates will differ ever so slightly; on larger phylogenies, the estimates generally seem to converge to each other – at least in my experience).
The terminology “Mk” refers to a Markov model with \(k\) states.
The other useful function is simmap, which simulates a stochastic character mapping on a tree given a Markov model generator matrix. This is the function that actually carries out steps 2-3 above.
A third function make.simmap, does both steps (fitMk and simmap) in one go. Obviously it is not necessary to invoke make.simmap after running fitMk.
Note: there are methods that exist (such as Delphy) that estimate paramters using a Markov chain Monte Carlo routine that explores the space of character mappings. This is not what simmap and make.simmap do. They simply use ace/fitMk results to simulate character mappings on trees.


Let’s refit our two Markov models with ace, and then do the same thing using fitMk from phytools. The function calls are very similar (but fitMk switches the ordering of tree and tip data):

# fitting models with ace:

fit1.ace <- ace(x, tree, type='discrete', model='ER')
fit2.ace <- ace(x, tree, type='discrete', model='ARD')

# fitting models with fitMk:

fit1.fitMk <- fitMk(tree, x, model="ER")
fit2.fitMk <- fitMk(tree, x, model="ARD")

How do the estimates for the ER model compare with the two methods?

fit1.ace$rates

[1] 0.0008592213

fit1.fitMk$rates

[1] 0.0008605929


The rate estimates are very close, but not exactly the same. The log-likelihood values at the MLE are also not identical:

fit1.ace$loglik

[1] -11.89298

fit1.fitMk$logLik

[1] -12.58613


The two functions, ace and fitMk, use slightly different likelihood functions, and different optimization routines to find the MLE. The fitMk function also allows users to supply a “root prior” different from a uniform distribution (which is the default in fitMk, see this post).
For large datasets, the choice of root prior in fitMk should (hopefully) not influence results too much.
For completeness, let’s compare the results of the ARD model with each method:

fit2.ace$rates

[1] 0.000000000 0.003628625

fit2.fitMk$rates

[1] 0.000000000 0.003628625


And the log-likelihoods:

fit2.ace$loglik

[1] -10.28796

fit2.fitMk$logLik

[1] -10.98111


So, the differences in the estimates themselves are negligible.



The phytools package simulates stochastic character mappings easily once we have a fitMk object. Notice that our fit1.fitMk we created above has everything we need already, including the tree:

names(fit1.fitMk)

 [1] "logLik"       "rates"        "index.matrix" "states"       "pi"          
 [6] "method"       "root.prior"   "opt_results"  "data"         "tree"        
[11] "lik"         


The matrix for our fitted Markov chain is encoded in rates, index.matrix, and states.
Simulating character mappings is easy - we just call the simmap (“simulate mapping”) function:

simmap.er <- simmap(fit1.fitMk)

Now, let’s plot some of these:

# specify the colors:
cols <- c("#F8766D","#00BFC4")
names(cols) <- c("P","S")

par(mfrow=c(3,3))

for(i in 1:9){ 
    plotSimmap(simmap.er[i], 
        ftype="off",
        colors=cols)
    }
mtext('ER model character mappings', side=3, line=-2, outer=TRUE,cex=1.7)








As you can see, these trees differ subtly in terms of the precise locations of amino acid changes along edges. Some of these have the root state residing in one state or the other.
What about the ARD model that estimated unidirectional change only?

# Simulate character mappings:
simmap.ard <- simmap(fit2.fitMk)

# specify the colors:
cols <- c("#F8766D","#00BFC4")
names(cols) <- c("P","S")

par(mfrow=c(3,3))

for(i in 1:9){ 
    plotSimmap(simmap.ard[i], 
        ftype="off",
        colors=cols)
    }
mtext('ARD model character mappings', side=3, line=-2, outer=TRUE,cex=1.7)








All of these character mappings assign the root state to the amino acid P. However, the collection of character mapping still has variable numbers of mutations across the different simulations. The majority of the time, the P to S mutation giving rise to the large blue clade in the upper part of the trees does appear to be happening pretty early with high probability.
We can also make some summary plots that include gradients in color on edges that describe average probabilities across the simulations (but getting our color scheme to work is a little tricky).

densityMap(simmap.er, ftype='off', main='ER model')


densityMap(simmap.ard, ftype='off', main='ARD model')

title: Introduction to BEAST2
text:
Introduction to BEAST2
For ease of navigation, I am including (most of) the critical information for the workshop on this website, but you may find it valuable to navigate to
taming-the-beast.org
which has a wealth of tutorials to help you navigate the BEAST2 ecosystem.
Several of the example datasets we are using are available in the Taming the Beast tutorials. Briefly, they are:

Primate mitochrondrial DNA (12 sequences, 898 sites, no location data – for intro BEAST2 runs only)
Influenza H3N2 (60 sequences, 1762 sites, 2 locations)
Influenza H5N1 (43 sequences, 1698 sites, 5 locations)
Bat Rabies (372 sequences, 1353 sites, 14 locations, actually from the BEASTX tutorials but we will use it anyway)
MERS (274 sequences, 30,130 sites, 2 host types – camel and human)

title: Workshop Requirements
text:
Phylogeography, broadly speaking, is concerned with reconstructing evolutionary histories to explain spatial distributions of present-day organisms (be they pathogens, species of mammals, or anything else that evolves). Many of the methods used in phylogeography are common to other areas of evolutionary biology, and some are useful for answering questions like “Does a particular trait influence speciation?” We will attempt to cover as many of these different areas as time allows, but our emphasis will be to work towards an understanding of the mathematical models underpinning these methods, with a particular focus on the key assumptions they make that render them appropriate for certain types of analyses and not others.
Prior experience in phylogenetics or evolution is not required or assumed, but enthusiasm is. Familiarity with statistics, Markov chains, R, and working in the terminal is great, but not strictly required. The target audience for this workshop includes all of the graduate students in MAGPIE, as well as anyone else who is interested to learn about mathematical models used in phylogeography and evolution of traits more generally.
Install all of the following things before Day 1 so that we can hit the ground running.



You need to have R on your machine. It is usually good to have the latest version. Download it at https://www.r-project.org/
You will need the following R packages:
ape
expm
phytools
coda
mcmcensemble
diversitree
These will have their own dependencies – be sure to install those as necessary in order to get these packages to work.
You can use Rstudio if you wish, but it is not required. If you are OK with copying-and-pasting things into a terminal, you will be able to accomplish all of the tasks in this workshop.
You will probably like to have a text editor to modify the coding exercises to work through problems efficiently. If you want to do this workshop on Expert mode, consider learning how to use Vim at https://vim-adventures.com



BEAST2 carries out Markov Chain Monte Carlo to reconstruct phylogenetic trees (and can do so jointly with geographic histories). There are a number of things you need to install to get this to work:
BEAST2 (running MCMC): download at https://www.beast2.org/
Tracer (analyzing MCMC): download at https://tree.bio.ed.ac.uk/software/tracer/
FigTree (examining trees produced by BEAST2): https://tree.bio.ed.ac.uk/software/figtree/
AliView (useful for looking at multiple sequence alignments): https://ormbunkar.se/aliview/



We will try to get BEAST2 to run on the Fir server. You will need an account with the Digital Research Alliance of Canada. Create one here if you don’t have one aready:
https://ccdb.alliancecan.ca/security/login

title: Exercise set 1:
text:
rm(list=ls())
library(coda) # for handling output from MCMC
library(phytools) # simmap functions, Mk models

This code is complementary to the other one in this subdirectory, and walks through how to fit models using functions from phytools.
Most of the phytools stuff is based on the Mk model, which is a particular type of Markov model. M stands for “Markov”, and “k” indicates the number of states. (Lewis has an interesting discussion relating to the use of conditional likelihoods as a means of overcoming acquisition bias – the tendency to only record changes of state, and not maintenance of a character).
fitMk fits the model to the data using nlminb, and optimization routine similar to quasi- Newton methods like BFGS. Compare this to ace.
mcmcMk fits Mk models to trees using MCMC.

data(sunfish.tree)
data(sunfish.data)

# extract discrete character (fish diet):
sunfish_tipdata <-setNames(sunfish.data$feeding.mode,
    rownames(sunfish.data))

# fit "ER" model
fit.ER<-fitMk(sunfish.tree,sunfish_tipdata,model="ER")
print(fit.ER)

This will run for a while, and you should see traceplots updating in real time until it finishes:

    fit.mcmc <- mcmcMk(sunfish.tree, sunfish_tipdata,
    model="ER", ngen=10000)

The dimensions of fit.mcmc will be ngen * (number of parameters in the Mk model + 2), which for this run will be 10000 x 3 because we are only estimating a single parameter
The first column of fit.mcmc is timesteps of the MCMC routine. The last column of fit.mcmc is the logLikelihood, and the columns between first and last are the parameters at each timestep.
We can look at the traceplots:

plot(fit.mcmc)

We can also plot the posterior distribution for our parameters. By default, this should toss out the first 20% of the MCMC updates as part of a burn-in (we discard transients because we only care about the stationary distribution of the MCMC run)

plot(density(fit.mcmc))

Of note, we can also use base R plotting to accomplish these things as well. Here is a histogram of the parameter values of the entire MCMC run:

hist(fit.mcmc[,'[pisc,non]'],breaks=100)

# and the last 80% of the run:
hist(fit.mcmc[c(2001:10000),'[pisc,non]'],breaks=100)

I also like to plot profile log- likelihood (logLik~parameters):

plot(fit.mcmc[,'logLik']~fit.mcmc[,'[pisc,non]'])

This helps us visualize the peak of the likelihood surface. Can be useful for diagnosing convergence problems.

title: Exercise set 1:
